references.notes: This verse refers to 'the speech of chickens' (منطق الدجاج) which was interpreted as an omen, a common form of augury (ṭīra) in pre-Islamic Arabia, influencing decisions like breaking a covenant.
The mention of the raven's shadow (ظل الغراب) approaching as a cause for fear explicitly refers to the pre-Islamic practice of augury and omens derived from birds.
The phrase 'نفس الفتى رهن بقمرة مؤرب' (the soul of the youth is pledged to a fateful night/destiny) refers to a pre-ordained fate or destiny, a key cosmological concept in pre-Islamic thought.
The term 'الدهر' (Time/Fate) is used here as an active, personified force that brings events, reflecting the pre-Islamic belief in dahr as an uncontrollable cosmological agent.
Similar to verse 13, 'الدهر' (Time/Fate) is depicted as an active agent that takes things away, reinforcing its role as a cosmological force in pre-Islamic belief.

The phrase 'رفعت بها أصوات نوح مسلب' (voices of lamentation of a bereaved woman were raised) explicitly refers to the ritualized mourning and wailing for the deceased, a common pre-Islamic mortuary practice.

The phrase 'لولا الإله' (Were it not for the Deity) refers to a divine entity, functioning as an invocation of divine power or will, consistent with pre-Islamic references to a high god or generic deity.
The verse refers to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as an unyielding, impersonal force that cannot be blamed, reflecting a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept of fate.
This verse contemplates the concept of the soul's immortality, which falls under the nature of death and the afterlife as conceptual content in pre-Islamic thought.
The term "المنون" (al-manūn) refers to fate or death as an irresistible, often personified, cosmological force in pre-Islamic belief.
The mention of "نوء السماك والأسد" (nawʾ al-simāk wa-l-asad) refers to astral belief, where the rising and setting of specific stars were associated with weather patterns and omens.
The reference to "مآتم" (maʾātim) explicitly denotes mourning gatherings, which are a part of pre-Islamic mortuary rites and beliefs.
This verse refers to the irresistible force of fate/existence ('ريب البرية') and the pre-Islamic belief in the absence of immortality ('لا خلودا'), both central cosmological concepts.
The verse explicitly mentions 'al-qadar' (fate/destiny) as an external force bringing about events, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological views on fate.
The term 'al-maʿbad' (place of worship/sanctuary) explicitly refers to a sacred space, fitting the category of Rituals_and_Spaces.
The verse explicitly mentions 'jinn' (supernatural beings), indicating a belief in such entities.
The term 'الدهر' (time/fate) is invoked as an active force that can prevent or not prevent, reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of Dahr as an agent of fate.
This verse explicitly refers to 'restraining your birds,' which is a direct allusion to the pre-Islamic practice of bird augury (ṭīra) for divination.
The term 'al-manāyā' (fates/deaths) refers to a pre-Islamic cosmological concept of an impersonal force governing destiny and death.
The phrase 'banāt al-dahr' (daughters of Time/Fate) personifies 'dahr' as a destructive cosmological force, a key pre-Islamic concept.
The term 'al-musahhar' (enchanted/bewitched) implies the influence of supernatural forces, typically associated with jinn or magic in pre-Islamic belief.

The term 'نوء' (nawʾ) refers to the pre-Islamic Arabian belief that the rising or setting of certain stars or constellations brings specific weather events or influences fate, clearly indicating an astral belief system.
This is an explicit oath formula common in pre-Islamic Arabic, invoking the life of an ancestor as a sacred entity to affirm a statement.
The phrase "حدث الدهر" refers to the events or vicissitudes brought about by "dahr," which in pre-Islamic thought was often personified as a cosmological force of fate or time.
This verse explicitly mentions wailing women lamenting, which is a common pre-Islamic mourning practice.
The personification of 'dahr' (time/fate) as a destructive force that betrays and causes ruin reflects a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of fate.
This verse explicitly prohibits specific actions (scratching the face, shaving hair) that were common, albeit sometimes forbidden, practices during pre-Islamic mourning rites.
This verse refers to the custom of mourning for a full year, which was a recognized duration for lamentation in pre-Islamic Arabian society.
The verse explicitly refers to 'the assembly of the jinn' (نادي الجن), indicating a belief in these supernatural entities common in pre-Islamic Arabia.
This verse attributes agency to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate), a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept where dahr was seen as an active, often destructive, force.
Similar to verse 2, this verse refers to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as an agent of events, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of fate.
The term 'manīya' (my destined death) refers to the pre-Islamic concept of an inescapable fate or destiny, a key cosmological idea.
This verse uses 'al-Manīya' (destined death) as an inevitable appointment, reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological belief in fate.
This verse again personifies 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as the agent of misfortune, consistent with pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs.
This phrase explicitly refers to practitioners of geomancy or pebble divination, a well-attested pre-Islamic divinatory practice.
This phrase explicitly refers to practitioners of augury from birds, a common pre-Islamic method of divination.
The plural 'al-Manāyā' (fates/destinies) refers to the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of inescapable forces determining life and death.
This verse refers to pre-Islamic mourning gatherings (ma'ātim) and the act of lamentation (nadb) for the deceased.
The phrase 'لا يبعد الله أربدا' is an invocation of Allāh, a known pre-Islamic deity, for blessing.
The term 'الدهر' (dahr) is used here to refer to time or fate as an active, often destructive, cosmological force.
The phrase 'خطوا له يوما من الأرض مضجعا' describes the act of preparing a burial place for the deceased.
The phrase 'لعمر أبيك الخير' is an oath formula invoking the life of a respected person, common in pre-Islamic speech.
The term 'المنون' (al-manūn) refers to fate or death as a cosmological force or personification of destiny.
The phrase 'لحا الله' is an invocation of Allāh to curse, demonstrating the deity's perceived power in pre-Islamic belief.
The term 'الدهر' (dahr) is again used to refer to time or fate as an active, often destructive, cosmological force, here being cursed.

The term 'rāqī' refers to a charmer or enchanter, a person who performs incantations (ruqya) to deal with snakes or other afflictions, indicating a specialized role involving interaction with the supernatural in pre-Islamic society.
This verse explicitly mentions 'الشيطين' (shayāṭīn/devils), which are non-divine supernatural entities in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The phrase "صبح الحرام" refers to the morning of a sacred or forbidden period, likely one of the pre-Islamic sacred months or a sacred territory.
The term "غول" explicitly refers to a ghoul, a type of jinn or demonic entity in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The word "حجة" refers to the pre-Islamic pilgrimage, a significant ritual practice.
The term "جنيا" explicitly refers to a jinn, a supernatural being in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The phrase "أرباب ربها" refers to multiple lords or deities, implying a polytheistic context and a ritualistic action towards them.
This verse describes the pre-Islamic burial practice of throwing large stones over a tomb (ḍarīḥ).
The mention of "الصيام" (fasting) refers to a ritual practice known in pre-Islamic Arabia.

This verse explicitly names several stars or constellations (Qarzāl, al-Jawūn, Taḥjal, al-Naʿāma, al-Khubāl), indicating a reference to pre-Islamic astral belief, which falls under cosmological concepts.
This phrase refers to the destructive power of time or fate, a central cosmological concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
This phrase refers to the destructive power of time or fate, a central cosmological concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
This phrase attributes destructive power to 'the nights,' echoing the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of time or fate as an irresistible force.
This verse refers to 'Tayman Dhū Ẓilāl', which is interpreted as a sacred geographical location or sanctuary, possibly a sacred grove, within the ancient city of Tayman.
The term 'الدهر' (time/fate) is invoked as a force that can admonish, reflecting its pre-Islamic cosmological significance as an agent of destiny.
The phrase 'أمك هابل' (your mother is Hubal's) is a pre-Islamic curse or expression of contempt, explicitly naming the deity Hubal.
The comparison of women to 'السعالي' (Saʿālī), a type of female jinn or ghoul, explicitly references a pre-Islamic supernatural being.
The comparison of riders to 'سعال' (Saʿālī), a type of female jinn or ghoul, explicitly references a pre-Islamic supernatural being.
The verse refers to 'the treachery of an inattentive dahr,' explicitly naming dahr as a cosmological force associated with fate or time in pre-Islamic belief.
The verse explicitly mentions 'الجني' (the jinn), referring to a non-divine supernatural entity in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The verse personifies 'الدهر' (Dahr) as looking upon and imploring, reinforcing its role as a cosmological force in pre-Islamic thought.
The term 'حريم' (ḥarīm) refers to a sacred, inviolable territory or sanctuary, a concept central to pre-Islamic Arabian religious geography and ritual spaces.

This phrase refers to the act of declaring something sacred (أهلّ) after the months of Jumada, likely indicating the entry into a sacred period or state, possibly related to pilgrimage or the sacred months (الأشهر الحرم) in pre-Islamic Arabian practice.
The verse explicitly mentions 'jinn' (جن) as a comparison, referring to supernatural entities believed to inhabit the Arabian Peninsula in pre-Islamic times.
These verses describe stopping at and observing a permanent grave, which relates to pre-Islamic burial practices and the remembrance of the deceased.
This verse explicitly refers to the act of killing nine individuals in revenge for Abū Lubaynā, which reflects the pre-Islamic tribal obligation of blood vengeance (tha'r).
The mention of Minā refers to a sacred site and pilgrimage destination in pre-Islamic Arabia.
This phrase refers to the pre-Islamic concept of sacred and profane months, where certain months were designated as forbidden (ḥaram) for warfare and other activities.
The mention of 'fasting' (ṣiyām) refers to a pre-Islamic ritual practice, often associated with vows or specific sacred periods.
The term 'أزلامها' (azlāmuhā) explicitly refers to divination arrows, a well-documented pre-Islamic practice for seeking omens or making decisions.
The phrase 'جن البدي' (jinn al-badī) directly names jinn, which are supernatural entities central to pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The 'جزور أيسار' (jazūr aysār) refers to a camel slaughtered for the pre-Islamic gambling ritual of maysir, which involved ritualistic distribution of meat and had sacrificial undertones.
The mention of a 'بيت' (house/temple) in الأفلاج, a region in Najd, likely refers to a pre-Islamic sanctuary or cultic structure, fitting the category of sacred spaces.

This verse contains the oath formula 'فلا وأبيك' (No, by your father), which is a common pre-Islamic oath sworn by a respected ancestor or figure, fitting the category of oath formulae anchored to a sacred entity.
This verse refers to 'al-dahr' (time/fate) as a powerful force that prevents immortality, reflecting a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The mention of 'al-ṣadā' (the bone-bird or spirit of the deceased) weeping is a direct reference to a specific pre-Islamic mortuary belief.
This verse explicitly mentions 'jinn of ʿAbqar,' referring to a supernatural entity associated with pre-Islamic Arabian belief and often linked to poetic inspiration.
This verse explicitly mentions the ṣadā, the pre-Islamic belief in a bird-like entity emerging from the skull of an unavenged slain person.
This verse refers to the passing of the sacred months (al-ashhur al-ḥurum), a pre-Islamic period of ritual prohibition against warfare.
This verse explicitly names al-Lāt, a major pre-Islamic Arabian goddess, through the tribal name Taym al-Lāt.
The term "الدهر" (Time/Fate) is used to denote a powerful, active force that brings hardship, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The verse explicitly mentions "أنواح" (lamentations) and "مأتم" (a mourning gathering), which are components of pre-Islamic mourning rites.
The term "محرما" (sacred/forbidden) refers to a designated sacred or inviolable zone, akin to a ḥaram, which is a pre-Islamic sacred space concept.
The verb "أقسما" (he swore an oath) indicates a formal oath-taking, a significant ritualistic act in pre-Islamic Arabian society, often implicitly invoking divine witness.
Similar to verse 12, "الدهر" is presented as an active, influential force shaping human experience, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of fate.
This verse explicitly mentions "التمائم" (amulets), which were worn in pre-Islamic Arabia for protection against supernatural harm, such as the evil eye or jinn.
This verse refers to seeking refuge in a sacred precinct (ḥaram), a key concept in pre-Islamic Arabian ritual and sacred geography.
The mention of the stars al-Farqadayn and Āl Naʿsh as 'eternal' reflects pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs about the enduring nature of celestial bodies.
This refers to the pre-Islamic belief that the soul of a deceased person could transform into a bird (ṣadā or hāma) that would hover over the grave.
The phrase 'طير لاشياح' explicitly refers to birds used for augury, a common pre-Islamic practice of divination.
These verses describe the physical act of burial and the construction of the grave, which are pre-Islamic mortuary practices.
This verse describes women wearing 'مسوح الشعر' (haircloth) as a specific mourning rite, common in pre-Islamic Arabian society.
